title: Friendship
subtitle: What the Bible Says About Friendship
summary: Scripture elevates friendship beyond casual acquaintance — modeling it through David and Jonathan, celebrating it in Proverbs, and culminating in Jesus calling his disciples friends rather than servants.
body:
The friendship between David and Jonathan is one of Scripture's most powerful relational portraits. Their souls were knit together, and Jonathan loved David as his own soul [verse:1sam.18.1]. This friendship cost Jonathan his claim to the throne — genuine friendship often requires sacrifice.

Proverbs provides concentrated wisdom on friendship. A friend loves at all times, and a brother is born for adversity [verse:prov.17.17]. Fair-weather companionship does not qualify as friendship in Scripture's economy. Iron sharpens iron, and one person sharpens another [verse:prov.27.17]. True friends make each other better, even through the friction of honest feedback.

Jesus redefined the friendship concept at the Last Supper. He told his disciples: I no longer call you servants, because a servant does not know his master's business. Instead, I have called you friends [verse:john.15.15]. The promotion from servant to friend was based on intimacy — he had shared everything the Father told him.

But Jesus also defined friendship's cost: greater love has no one than this, that someone lay down his life for his friends [verse:john.15.13]. Friendship in the biblical imagination is not convenience. It is covenant.

Ecclesiastes provides the practical case for friendship: two are better than one, because if either falls, the other can help [verse:eccl.4.9-10]. We were not designed to navigate life alone.

verses.annotation: David and Jonathan — souls knit together in a friendship that cost everything.
Jesus elevates his disciples from servants to friends — intimacy through shared knowledge.
A friend loves at all times — constancy as the measure of genuine friendship.
Iron sharpens iron — friends make each other better through honest engagement.
The greatest love: laying down one's life for friends — friendship as sacrifice.
Two are better than one — the practical case for friendship.
There is a friend who sticks closer than a brother — chosen loyalty exceeding biological obligation.

scholars.quote: Bergen observes that Jonathan's friendship with David was an act of extraordinary selflessness — by supporting David, Jonathan knowingly surrendered his own succession to the throne, making their bond one of the most costly in Scripture.

Longman notes that Ecclesiastes 4:9-10 grounds friendship not in sentiment but in practical survival — the sage's argument is that isolated people are fundamentally more vulnerable than those who walk together.

Keener explains that Jesus' declaration 'I call you friends' in John 15 broke ancient patron-client conventions — in the Greco-Roman world, those below rarely received the status of 'friend' from someone above.
